stories.headline: Denver shuts down Ultra Lounge East after mass shooting, citing pattern of violence and licensing violations
stories.summary:
City licensing officials moved Wednesday to immediately suspend the liquor and entertainment licenses of Ultra Lounge East, a southeast Denver nightclub at 9755 E. Hampden Ave., three days after a gunman or gunmen fired 66 shots inside the club, killing a 22-year-old man and wounding eight others. The order from the Department of Licensing and Consumer Protection requires the club to cease operations and close immediately.

The suspension cites three violent incidents at the property in the past five months, including an earlier homicide just outside the club in March. Officials also found the club was serving food and hookah without proper licenses, and that the man currently operating the club, Dionte Wilkins, had not properly obtained the liquor and entertainment licenses from the original license holder.

Licensing manager Molly Duplechian wrote in the order that Wilkins may be ineligible for a liquor license due to his criminal history and allegedly tried to circumvent the licensing process by presenting himself as a manager rather than an owner. Wilkins said in a text message that he 'definitely did nothing purposely' and declined further comment.

The building's owner, Dol Bhattarai, told The Denver Post he leased the property to Wilkins about a year ago after running the Yak and Yeti restaurant there for a decade. Bhattarai said Wilkins had not paid rent for four months and that he had initiated eviction proceedings earlier this week.

stories.headline: Motorcyclist killed in Commerce City wrong-way crash
stories.summary:
A motorcyclist died early Wednesday morning after traveling the wrong direction on Colorado Boulevard and being struck by a car near E. 52nd Avenue in Commerce City, police said. The crash occurred around 1:20 a.m.

The driver of the car remained on scene and is cooperating with investigators from the Commerce City Police Department Traffic Team. The on-ramp in the 5300 block of Colorado Boulevard was closed while the investigation was conducted.

No further details about the identity of the motorcyclist or the circumstances leading to the wrong-way travel were immediately released. Police asked other drivers to avoid the area during the investigation.

stories.headline: Video of ICE detention at Denver International Airport draws national attention
stories.summary:
A widely circulated video shows federal agents removing a 27-year-old Ecuadorian woman, Chantal Alejandra Morales Rojas, from a Southwest Airlines jetway at Denver International Airport on Monday before leading her across the tarmac as bystanders shouted 'Help us! ICE is here!' The Department of Homeland Security confirmed to The Denver Post that U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents arrested Rojas, saying she entered the country in October 2024 and overstayed a visa that expired January 4, 2025.

Rojas, who lives in Oakland, California, and holds a work permit according to a GoFundMe page created on her behalf, had traveled to Aurora to spend time with a family she previously worked for as an au pair, attending the USA Ultimate Masters Championships. She was attempting to board a return flight to Oakland when agents approached her at the gate.

Alicia Dantzker, who started the GoFundMe and was traveling with Rojas, said agents did not present an arrest warrant to a lawyer who was standing outside the jetway door. Dantzker began filming the encounter and made as much noise as possible to draw attention. The crowdfunding campaign had raised more than $99,000 by Wednesday evening.

Rojas remains in ICE custody pending removal proceedings, a DHS spokesperson said. The incident has drawn significant public reaction both locally and nationally.

stories.headline: AG Phil Weiser names Jefferson County Commissioner Lesley Dahlkemper as running mate in Colorado governor's race
stories.summary:
Colorado Attorney General Phil Weiser announced Wednesday that Jefferson County Commissioner Lesley Dahlkemper will be his running mate in the November gubernatorial election. Weiser made the announcement at a news conference in downtown Denver, calling Dahlkemper 'an extraordinary asset' with a record of taking on complex public problems.

Dahlkemper has served on the Jefferson County Commission since 2018 and was reelected in 2022. She previously served on the Jefferson County Public Schools board, including as its president, and worked as a journalist at Colorado Public Radio and in public relations before entering politics.

Dahlkemper is married to Mike Feeley, a former state Senate Democratic leader who is also a shareholder at the Denver law and lobbying firm Brownstein Hyatt Farber Schreck. She cited her work with Weiser's office on the opioid crisis as evidence of his leadership. She declined to say whether she would draw dual salaries as lieutenant governor, an arrangement that has drawn criticism in the current administration.

Weiser faces Republican Victor Marx, a Marine veteran and evangelical Christian ministry leader, in the general election. Marx has not yet named a running mate.

stories.headline: Polis forms Competitiveness Council as Colorado economy sheds jobs and rankings slip
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis announced Wednesday the formation of a new Governor's Competitiveness Council, a public-private partnership designed to reverse Colorado's declining economic standing and stem ongoing job losses. The council, which includes business leaders and state and local economic development officials, is tasked with analyzing the current business landscape and delivering legislative and policy recommendations before Polis leaves office in January.

Colorado's nonfarm employment has fallen by approximately 9,100 jobs since peaking in June 2024, with the information sector — a key indicator of technological innovation — down 19 percent from its recent high, a steeper drop than the 11 percent national decline. The state's labor force has also shrunk by more than 80,000 people since October 2024, limiting employers' ability to expand.

Polis acknowledged that Colorado has slipped toward the middle of the pack in national competitiveness rankings, and said rising housing costs are a primary factor hampering the state's ability to recruit workers. The council's findings will be shared with all gubernatorial candidates and the state legislature.

The move follows an April letter from more than 260 business leaders, organized under the group Engage Colorado, warning that the state was losing its reputation as a hub for startups, innovation, and venture capital. The University of Colorado Boulder's Leeds Business Research Division will assist with data analysis and benchmarking.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis sets personal veto record, breaks with own party on 12 bills in 2026 session
stories.summary:
Despite Democrats holding majorities in both chambers of the Colorado General Assembly, Gov. Jared Polis vetoed 12 bills during the 2026 legislative session, surpassing his previous personal record of 11 vetoes. The decisions underscore Polis' pattern of operating independently from his own party on certain policy matters.

Among the most controversial vetoes was SB 26-005, the 'Rights Violation in Immigration Enforcement Remedy' bill, which was among the first legislation introduced in the session. The veto drew strong criticism from progressive Democrats who supported the measure.

The veto record reflects ongoing tensions between Polis and the left wing of the state Democratic Party on issues including immigration, business regulation, and fiscal policy. Polis has consistently positioned himself as a centrist or libertarian-leaning Democrat throughout his tenure.

stories.headline: Aer Lingus to end nonstop Denver-Dublin service after September 28
stories.summary:
Aer Lingus will discontinue its nonstop service between Denver International Airport and Dublin Airport after September 28 as part of a broader cost-cutting restructuring plan. The Irish airline cited increased competitor capacity, rising fuel costs, growing seasonality of its business, and first-quarter losses of approximately $117 million as reasons for the cuts. The restructuring also includes a 6 percent reduction in overall flight capacity and the elimination of 500 jobs globally.

The Denver-Dublin route launched in May 2024, marking Aer Lingus' first-ever service to Colorado. Denver International Airport estimated at the time that the route would generate more than $65 million in annual economic impact and support more than 400 jobs in the state.

Passengers with affected bookings will be contacted directly by the airline or through the travel agent or tour operator used to purchase tickets, and will be offered reaccommodation or refund options. The airline said it remains committed to improving its operating margin and will retrofit 10 Airbus A330 cabins and introduce Premium Economy seating in 2027.

Colorado travelers wishing to reach Ireland will need to connect through other hubs once the route ends. Aer Lingus CEO Lynne Embleton called the restructuring an 'accelerated transformation' to position the airline for long-term competitiveness in transatlantic travel.

stories.headline: Denver-area venture capital investment falls by more than half in second quarter
stories.summary:
Venture capital investment in the Denver and Boulder areas dropped by more than 50 percent in the second quarter of 2026 compared to the prior year, according to data reported by the Denver Business Journal. Local firms also face record lows in fundraising, compounding concerns about the health of Colorado's startup and innovation economy.

The decline comes amid broader warnings from the business community about Colorado's slipping competitiveness. A letter signed by more than 260 business leaders earlier this year cited shrinking access to capital as one of the key challenges facing Colorado entrepreneurs.

The pullback in venture investment follows a national trend of tighter capital markets, but Colorado's drop appears steeper than national averages, adding pressure on state officials who have made innovation economy growth a central policy priority.

stories.headline: FirstBank signage to disappear from Denver area as PNC merger integration moves forward
stories.summary:
Familiar FirstBank name and logos on buildings across the Denver metro area will soon be removed following the completion of a $4.1 billion acquisition by PNC Financial Services Group that closed earlier this year. The rebranding will significantly expand PNC's physical and customer footprint in the Denver market.

FirstBank was one of Colorado's largest and most recognizable banking institutions, with deep roots in the Front Range community. The transition marks a significant shift in the local banking landscape for both retail and commercial customers.

PNC has been expanding aggressively in fast-growing Sun Belt and Mountain West markets. Denver's conversion is part of a broader integration effort that is expected to roll out over coming months.

stories.headline: Colorado Parks and Wildlife confirms fourth wolf pack, this one in Eagle County
stories.summary:
Colorado Parks and Wildlife announced Wednesday the formation of a fourth established wolf pack in the state, naming the new family group the Piney Ridge Pack after its location in Eagle County. The confirmation means four packs have successfully reproduced in 2026, a significant milestone for the wolf reintroduction program that began in 2023.

The other three confirmed packs are the One Ear Pack in Jackson County, the Three Creeks Pack in Rio Blanco County, and the Copper Creek Pack, the first to form in Colorado after reintroduction. Most of the Copper Creek Pack was captured and relocated to Pitkin County after members killed livestock in Grand County.

CPW wolf program manager Eric Odell said officials are continuing to monitor all four packs and working to count pups, with a full minimum population count to be released in the agency's annual spring report. Research has found that wolves in established packs have significantly higher survival rates than lone wolves, and pack formation helps wildlife managers focus conflict mitigation resources in specific territories.

Of the 10 wolves that died between April 2025 and March 2026, seven were dispersed individuals and three were pack members. CPW is encouraging residents who spot wolves to submit sighting forms on the agency's website.

stories.headline: Nuggets decline team option on Jalen Pickett, adding to unsettled offseason roster questions
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets have chosen to decline the team option on guard Jalen Pickett, according to league sources cited by The Denver Post. The move makes Pickett an unrestricted free agent ahead of the 2026-27 season.

The decision adds to an already uncertain offseason for Denver, which still has restricted free agents Peyton Watson and Spencer Jones unsigned. The Nuggets have filled several roster spots with signings including Tyus Jones, Marvin Bagley III, Alpha Diallo, and rookies Trevon Brazile and Bryce Hopkins, but three roster spots remain open.

Denver is navigating significant cap constraints as it weighs whether to meet a projected $25 million per season asking price for Watson. The team finished the 2026 NBA Summer League with a 3-2 record. Coming off a first-round playoff exit, the Nuggets face increasing pressure to reshape the roster around Nikola Jokic and Jamal Murray.

stories.headline: Colorado Avalanche sign forward Georgii Merkulov to one-year deal for 2026-27 season
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche have signed forward Georgii Merkulov to a one-year contract for the upcoming 2026-27 NHL season. Merkulov adds depth to an Avalanche forward group that has been active this offseason.

Merkulov, who has played in both the NHL and AHL, gives Colorado additional roster flexibility heading into training camp. Financial terms of the deal were not immediately disclosed.

The signing continues Colorado's offseason roster construction as the team looks to remain competitive in the Central Division.

joke.joke: You know you're an adult when you get excited about a new sponge.

topStory.headline: U.S. Signs Nuclear Cooperation Agreement With Saudi Arabia, Opening Path to Domestic Uranium Enrichment
topStory.summary:
The United States and Saudi Arabia signed a civilian nuclear cooperation agreement Wednesday that could allow the kingdom to enrich uranium on its own soil. U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright and Saudi Energy Minister Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman signed both a peaceful nuclear cooperation agreement and an accompanying bilateral safeguards agreement, according to the Department of Energy. The deal is expected to span 30 years and involve American companies including Westinghouse in building out the Saudi nuclear energy program.

Under the reported terms, Saudi Arabia could be permitted to construct a uranium enrichment facility following a joint study with the United States, rather than relying on imported nuclear fuel. Full details of the agreement have not yet been disclosed to Congress, which must review the deal and retains the authority to disapprove it. Experts say it could take at least a decade before Saudi Arabia brings a nuclear power plant online.

The agreement drew immediate criticism on Capitol Hill. At a House Foreign Affairs Committee hearing Wednesday, Rep. Brad Sherman, D-Calif., warned that the transfer of nuclear technology to Saudi Arabia carried long-term risks, drawing a parallel to U.S. arms sales to the Shah of Iran before the 1979 Islamic Revolution. Sherman called for an oversight hearing and said any deal should include the same nonproliferation safeguards contained in a 2009 U.S. agreement with the United Arab Emirates, under which Abu Dhabi voluntarily renounced domestic enrichment and reprocessing. Sherman also noted that he and then-Sen. Marco Rubio had introduced legislation in 2018 requiring congressional approval before any such agreement could be reached with Riyadh.

The announcement carries particular sensitivity given the United States is currently engaged in military action against Iran in part over Tehran's domestic uranium enrichment program. Critics, including Rosemary Kelanic of Defense Priorities, argued the timing was reckless and could pressure Iran to accelerate efforts to acquire a nuclear weapon. Saudi Arabia has previously stated it would seek nuclear weapons capability if Iran obtained one. The deal also lacks the enhanced International Atomic Energy Agency monitoring protocols that had been applied to Iran under the 2015 nuclear agreement.

The proposal has been under discussion across multiple administrations. The Biden administration had sought to tie Saudi access to nuclear technology to normalization of relations with Israel, a condition Saudi Arabia refused. The Trump administration moved forward without that condition. The agreement now goes to Congress, where it is expected to face opposition from lawmakers concerned about the absence of stronger nonproliferation commitments from Riyadh.
